project.title: The Three Soldiers — Vietnam Veterans Memorial
project.description: High-resolution 3D capture of *The Three Soldiers* (also known as *The Three Servicemen*), a bronze sculpture by Frederick Hart located on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. The statue depicts three young American servicemen — one White, one African American, and one Hispanic — and was unveiled on Veterans Day 1984 as a figurative complement to Maya Lin's abstract memorial wall.

related_objects.title: Vietnam Women's Memorial
related_objects.description: Bronze sculpture by Glenna Goodacre, dedicated 1993, located 300 ft south of The Wall.

provenance.operator: Michael Torres, National Mall Survey Team
provenance.processing_notes: Captured across two sessions (morning and afternoon) for optimal lighting. 1,240 images aligned, dense cloud cleaned of tourist occlusion, mesh decimated for web delivery.

quality_metrics.scale_verification: Verified against known plinth dimensions (base width 2.44 m).

data_quality.coverage_gaps: Minor occlusion under rifle sling and between figures at rear contact points.
data_quality.reconstruction_areas: Small infill on underside of helmet brims.
data_quality.color_calibration: X-Rite ColorChecker used, white balance corrected per session.

archival_record.title: The Three Soldiers (The Three Servicemen)

physical_description.condition: Good — characteristic green-brown patina with high-touch polish on hands and faces from visitor contact.

archival_record.provenance: Commissioned by the Vietnam Veterans Memorial Fund. Unveiled November 9, 1984. Administered by the National Park Service.

rights.credit_line: National Mall Survey Team, 2026. Sculpture by Frederick Hart, 1984.

context.description: The sculpture stands in a small grove of trees approximately 120 ft from the west end of The Wall (Vietnam Veterans Memorial). The three figures face The Wall, creating a symbolic connection between the figurative soldiers and the 58,318 names inscribed on the black granite panels. Hart's sculpture was added as a compromise after controversy over Maya Lin's abstract design.
context.location_history: Installed at the current site on the National Mall, November 9, 1984. Has not been relocated.

preservation.rendering_requirements: WebGL 2.0 with EXT_color_buffer_float for Gaussian splat rendering.
preservation.rendering_notes: Splat default orientation is rotated 180 degrees on X-axis (Z-up to Y-up conversion).

scene_0._source_notes: Trained for 50k iterations from 1,240 images. Two lighting conditions blended.

mesh_0._source_notes: Decimated from 180M to 8M faces. PBR textures baked at 8K.

pointcloud_0._source_notes: Registered from 8 scan positions around the sculpture group.

annotations.title: Polished contact area — lead figure's right hand
annotations.body: Surface patina worn to bright bronze from decades of visitor contact. Demonstrates public interaction with the memorial.

annotations.title: Orientation toward The Wall
annotations.body: All three figures face east-southeast toward the Vietnam Veterans Memorial Wall. This sightline is a deliberate design element by Frederick Hart.

version_history.description: Added afternoon session data for improved coverage of rear contact points.
